Precipitation extremes are likely to become more extreme (in both directions), seasonality is likely to become less predictable, and increased temperatures mean that droughts and floods are both likely to hit harder when they arrive. At the same time, the patterns of precipitation in Madagascar will remain dominated by year-to-year sources of variability such as the El Nino Southern Oscillation (ENSO) and Indian Ocean Dipole (IOD).

There is thus a need to better understand how communities can adapt to such variability today, in order to prepare for a more volatile climate tomorrow.

The scenario exercises presented in this report are an attempt at translating that understanding into actionable adaptation decisions at the community level. We have found that when climate risks and choices are presented clearly and honestly, rural communities are more likely to make the adaptation decisions that are suitable to them^8.

With that motivation in mind, this section presents an overview of the state of the science on likely changes to the climate in Madagascar, drawing on the World Bank Climate Change Knowledge Portal^9 as well as a review of the academic literature.

docs.title: 2 Likely Changes to Heat and Precipitation
2.1 Climate Change Effects
docs.text:
Anthropogenic climate change is likely to increase average temperatures and shorten the length of the rainy season^10 over southern Madagascar^11.

Projected mean temperature changes, RCP4.5 (World Bank Climate Knowledge Portal)

Projected changes in length of primary rainy season (Dunning et al 2018)

These changes are linked to shifts in the position of the tropical rain belt (ITCZ) which occur in most model projections^12. These changes are likely to translate to lower moisture availability for crops and rangeland^13. The fingerprint of these changes is already detectable in recent years^14; however, their full extent is not likely to emerge until the middle of the century and the long-term magnitude of the changes is highly uncertain.

We may observe unprecedented low and high extremes in rainfall as a result of climate change, due to the increasing moisture capacity of the air under warming as well as the potential ways in which climate change may act as a multiplier on existing sources of decadal climate variability^15. In southern Madagascar, we observe the greatest potential for unprecedented extreme precipitation events (in both directions) around Dec-Jan. These predicted extreme conditions are similar to those observed during communities’ worst reported droughts in the recent past, and coincide with the rice planting season^16. We are also likely to see an increase in the possibility of temperatures hazardous for human health (>35c) during the austral summer.

Projected changes in extreme temperatures, RCP4.5 (World Bank Climate Knowledge Portal)

Projected medium-term variability in rainfall, RCP4.5 (World Bank Climate Knowledge Portal)

docs.title: 2.2 Decadal Processes
docs.text:
At the same time that we observe emerging changes in the long-term patterns of precipitation and temperature as a result of climate change, rainfall in southern Madagascar remains dominated by year-to-year patterns of variability^17.

The most significant of those are IOD and ENSO^18. Both affect precipitation over southern Madagascar, but ENSO has a greater contribution in the southwest, while IOD has a greater contribution in the southeast. IOD has a strong effect on both extreme high and low precipitation events^19, while ENSO mainly affects the likelihood of drought (greater during an El Nino year)^20. Many of communities’ reported worst droughts in the recent past were ENSO events. At the same time, while ENSO and IOD may act as limiting factors on total available seasonal precipitation, they are only somewhat related with subannual dry^21 and wet spells^22. We can observe this in the fact that only some of communities’ recollected worst droughts were during overall dry seasons. When ENSO and IOD interact, the likelihood of droughts like the major one in 2019-2021 increase^23 greatly.

Relationship between rainfall and IOD (Randiratsara et. al. 2022)

Relationship between rainfall and ENSO (Randriamahefasoa and Reason, 2017)

ENSO and IOD, and their resulting effects on seasonal total precipitation over southern Madagascar, can be predicted with decent skill 1-3 months in advance. DGM is working with Columbia to develop tailored drought alert forecasts based on this principle.

Seasonal forecast skill for OND and NDJ (DGM & Azhar Ehsan, Columbia)

Correlation of dry spells (left) and wet spells (right) frequency with total seasonal precipitation (DGM & Azhar Ehsan, Columbia)

ENSO and IOD have been observed to have major impacts on crop and rangeland productivity in southern Madagascar^24. Seasonal decreases in soil moisture are also likely to have an effect on water availability for irrigation, and thus reduce the productivity of rice^25.

ENSO has also been observed to have an impact on vector-bourne diseases in Madagascar. The main observed pathway for this effect is in the increase in livestock migration during droughts^26. On the other hand, flooding during wet years is also likely linked to an increase in vector-bourne disease outbreak, but these extremes are less predictable on a seasonal timescale.

docs.title: 3.1 Climate Change Effects
docs.text:
The impact of anthropogenic climate change on cyclone incidence and intensity in Madagascar is uncertain.

There is not clear consensus across models and scenarios on either aspect of projected cyclone hazard. At the same time, that does not mean that the likeliest project outcome is no change. Instead, it means that there are a few different potential regional storylines of how climate change will affect cyclones, which point in countervailing directions.

Projected changes in storm incidence, RCP4.5, 2035-2064 (World Bank Climate Knowledge Portal)

Per Mindlin et al (2020)^27, the two main drivers of potential changes in cyclone incidence are “the enhanced warming of the tropical upper troposphere” on the one hand, and “the strengthening of the stratospheric polar vortex” on the other. Mindlin et al (2020) finds that in storylines of potential climate change effects, “the response to tropical warming leads to a strengthening of the midlatitude westerly winds, whilst the response to a delayed breakdown (for DJF) or strengthening (for JJA) of the stratospheric vortex leads to a poleward shift of the westerly winds and the storm tracks”. Together these two drivers explain 50-60% of cyclone variance in the region. In the first storyline, the incidence of cyclones in the region increases, while in the second, it decreases. These storylines are associated with a strong increase or weak decrease in overall DJF precipitation, respectively, while JJA precipitation weakly decreases under all storylines.

Diverging storylines for future cyclone density - (a) troposphere warming, (b) stratospheric vortex, (c) correlation between between SV and storm density (Mindlin et al 2020)

In addition to the incidence and intensity of storms, climate change may affect their potential geographic reach as well^28. Per ongoing work from the Red Cross REPRESA project^29, potential storylines of unprecedented extremes include the following:

  • An average strong storm forecasted with a wide range of possible landfall locations, at the 7-day lead times needed for prepositioning.
  • A storm initially forecasted to not be strong enough to reach the trigger, but then at a shorter lead-time rapidly intensified
  • A storm forecasted to make two landfalls, the first in the northeast more certain, the second in the southwest more uncertain, but with a more vulnerable population.

Cyclones not only impact property and crops, but also have multidimensional effects on food insecurity and health. Community self-help has the potential to mitigate these impacts^30.

docs.title: 3.2 Decadal Processes
docs.text: Cyclones are less directly tied to seasonal sources of variability than precipitation is. At the same time, these processes do have some effect on cyclone formation. Per Matyas (2014)^31, “More TCs formed in the equatorward (poleward) sections of the channel when [IOD was] negative (positive). Trajectories of TCs forming north of 18° S tended to be curved and initially move south or southwest while experiencing easterly vertical wind shear and higher SSTs while those south of 18° S moved fairly straight from west to east over lower SSTs and strong westerly vertical wind shear. Landfall tended to occur over Madagascar when the MJO was in position to enhance cyclogenesis over the channel… ENSO did not exhibit statistically significant relationships with formation frequency, location, or track shape.”

docs.title: 4. How Can Community Feedback and Science Inform Each Other
docs.text:
Community assessments can tell us what important aspects of climate change to focus on. At the same time, the science can also tell us how to talk about potential climate change scenarios with communities, and how to stress-test their potential effects^32.

What effects of climate extremes have you observed in southern Madagascar? Do you see ways in which these extremes are getting worse, or less predictable? What do these extreme events mean for the community-led adaptation possibilities we identified on day 1?

Increased bad year losses mean that there is a greater need to rely on a range of adaptation strategies to produce more in a good year, and manage risk. The next section breaks down those impacts and opportunities on the community scale.

docs.title: 3b. Understanding Community Climate Impacts in Madagascar
5. Recap of climate science analysis
docs.text:
Madagascar's climate risks are highly heterogeneous, with different regions exposed to distinct hazard profiles shaped by geography, atmospheric circulation, and oceanic influences. According to analyses from the Integrated Food Security Phase Classification (IPC)^1, climate shocks remain among the most important drivers of livelihood disruptions across the country, affecting agricultural production, livestock systems, natural resources, and rural economies. Furthermore, IPC emphasizes that acute food insecurity in the Grand Sud and Grand Sud-Est “is driven by a combination of recurring climate shocks (including droughts, floods, and cyclones).” Because much of the population relies on rain-fed subsistence agriculture, and climate-dependent pastoralism and fisheries, the island's hyper-regionalized climate directly governs these sector outputs.

The Potsdam Institute for Climate Impact Research^2 identified three broad climate-risk zones that are particularly relevant for understanding vulnerability and adaptation pathways in Madagascar. The semi-arid Grand Sud is particularly exposed to recurrent droughts and prolonged dry spells, while the Grand Sud-Est faces frequent cyclones, flooding, and coastal hazards associated with the Indian Ocean. Northern and northwestern regions are strongly influenced by monsoonal rainfall systems, where delayed rainfall onset, uneven precipitation, and cyclone-related flooding can disrupt agricultural production. Despite these regional differences, a common challenge across Madagascar is increasing climate variability, which affects rainfall timing, water availability, agricultural productivity, livestock systems, fisheries, and natural resource-dependent livelihoods.

Several studies, including Climate change linked to drought in Southern Madagascar^3, suggest that climate change may increase drought severity in the southern region through rising temperatures, increased evapotranspiration, and longer dry periods. While projected changes in average rainfall remain uncertain, warmer conditions are expected to intensify the impacts of rainfall deficits when droughts occur. Recent drought episodes in the Grand Sud illustrate how repeated dry years can compound livelihood losses and reduce recovery capacity.

From an adaptation planning perspective, the most important challenge may not be changes in average climate conditions, but rather the possibility that droughts, cyclones, and other climate shocks become more frequent, more intense, or occur in closer succession. Understanding how communities respond to climate variability today provides an important foundation for evaluating adaptation options under future climate conditions.

docs.title: 6. Farmer Input / Community Data
docs.text: The community climate survey revealed important differences between Manakara, Toliara I, and Toliara II, reflecting distinct livelihood systems, climate hazards, and vulnerability profiles. While drought emerged as the most frequently reported hazard across all communities, the frequency of bad years over the past decade and their livelihood impacts varied considerably according to local ecological and socio-economic conditions.

docs.title: Agricultural Communities – Manakara
docs.text: Manakara communities are predominantly dependent on rice, maize, cassava, and yam production. Over the past ten years, communities reported approximately 6 bad years out of 10 (6/10) associated with droughts, excessive rainfall, and cyclones. The most frequently cited bad years were 2019, 2020, 2021, 2022, and 2023, with 2022 identified as the most severe year due to the combined effects of drought and Cyclones Batsirai and Emnati. Droughts occurring between January and mid-February affected yam planting and rice establishment, while excessive rainfall in March and May disrupted maize production and rice flowering. Cyclones caused widespread crop destruction, increased crop diseases, and reduced household food security. Consequently, agriculture in Manakara exhibits a high sensitivity to both rainfall deficits and excess rainfall during critical crop growth stages. The overall frequency of reported agricultural bad years can therefore be estimated at approximately 60% of the last decade (6/10 years).

docs.title: Pastoral and Agro-Pastoral Communities – Toliara I
docs.text: Toliara I communities depend largely on livestock production, agro-pastoralism, and maize cultivation. Drought was overwhelmingly identified as the dominant hazard, with communities reporting approximately 7 bad years out of 10 (7/10) due to recurring rainfall deficits. The most frequently cited bad years included 2016, 2018, 2019, 2020, 2021, and 2022, reflecting the prolonged drought conditions that affected southern Madagascar. Drought resulted in reduced pasture availability, declining water resources, livestock migration, reduced herd productivity, and lower maize yields. Although excess rainfall and cyclones such as Cyclone Haruna (2013) were mentioned, they occurred less frequently than droughts. Compared to the other study sites, Toliara I exhibited the highest frequency of climate-related bad years because of its strong dependence on climate-sensitive rangelands. The overall frequency of reported bad years is estimated at approximately 70% of the last decade (7/10 years).

docs.title: Coastal Fishing Communities – Toliara II
docs.text: Toliara II communities are characterized by a strong dependence on fisheries, coastal resources, salt production, and small-scale trade. Communities reported approximately 5 bad years out of 10 (5/10), with cyclones and coastal storms representing the most significant hazards. The most frequently cited bad years were 2013 (Cyclone Haruna), 2022 (Cyclones Batsirai and Emnati), and 2023 (Cyclone Freddy). Unlike Toliara I, drought impacts were largely indirect, affecting water availability and coastal ecosystems rather than directly reducing fish catches. Cyclones caused extensive damage to fishing boats, fishing gear, landing sites, coastal infrastructure, and mangrove ecosystems that support fisheries productivity. Excess rainfall during January and February occasionally disrupted fishing activities and local markets. Overall, climate-related bad years in Toliara II were less frequent than in Toliara I but generally produced more severe episodic impacts when major cyclones occurred. The estimated frequency of bad years is approximately 50% of the last decade (5/10 years).

docs.title: Comparative Frequency and Livelihood Impacts
docs.text:
A comparison across the three livelihood systems shows that Toliara I recorded the highest frequency of bad years (7/10 years) due to recurrent drought and rangeland degradation. Manakara recorded approximately 6/10 bad years, reflecting its exposure to multiple hazards including droughts, floods, and cyclones that affect crop production. Toliara II recorded approximately 5/10 bad years, with major losses concentrated around severe cyclone years. While drought was the most recurrent hazard across all communities, cyclones generated the greatest livelihood losses when they occurred. Agriculture was most vulnerable in Manakara, pastoralism in Toliara I, and fisheries and coastal livelihoods in Toliara II. These findings highlight the need for livelihood-specific anticipatory action and adaptation strategies tailored to each community’s dominant hazard profile and seasonal risk calendar.

**Bad Year Frequency Ratio (Last 10 Years)**
Community Main Livelihood System Estimated Bad Years Ratio Manakara Agriculture 6 years 6/10 Toliara I Pastoralism / Agro-pastoralism 7 years 7/10 Toliara II Fisheries / Coastal Livelihoods 5 years 5/10

docs.title: 7. Madagascar National Adaptation Plan and MRCS operational approaches
docs.text:
The climate risks identified in the literature and echoed by communities across southern Madagascar have increasingly shaped national adaptation priorities. Recurrent droughts, cyclones, rainfall variability, land degradation, and pressure on climate-sensitive livelihoods have highlighted the need for adaptation approaches that strengthen both resilience and risk management. In response, Madagascar’s National Adaptation Plan (NAP)^4 identifies 3 priority sector actions: agriculture and food security, forestry and biodiversity, and water and health. The NAP promotes a combination of climate-smart agriculture, sustainable natural resource management, ecosystem-based adaptation, climate information services, and strengthened disaster risk governance. 

Many of these priorities are reflected in the operational approaches implemented by the Malagasy Red Cross Society (MRCS)^5. Through partnerships with government agencies, IFRC, and development actors, MRCS supports community-based interventions that combine disaster preparedness, anticipatory action, livelihood resilience, and ecosystem restoration. Current activities include early warning dissemination, simulation exercises, forecast-based planning, livelihood diversification initiatives, climate-smart agriculture, fisheries support, and nature-based solutions such as mangrove restoration and coastal reforestation.

Together, the NAP and MRCS programming reflect a growing recognition that adaptation must address both long-term climate change and recurrent climate variability. However, many adaptation measures require time, resources, and favorable conditions to generate benefits. As climate shocks become more frequent or occur in closer succession, there is increasing interest in understanding how these interventions perform under stress and whether additional risk-management approaches may be needed to protect livelihoods and support recovery

docs.title: 7.1  Case Study 1 — Agriculture + Agroforestry
docs.text:
Tabulation: Climate Hazards Affecting Agricultural Livelihoods

Climate Hazard Evidence from Climate Analysis Community Feedback Adaptation Plan Drought CHIRPS analysis shows Jan–mid‑Feb rainfall deficits in Manakara during worst drought years; overlaps with yam planting and early rice growth. Seasonal drought in Toliara driven by late onset, early cessation, and short dry spells. Farmers report failure of rice seedlings and yam plantings when early‑season rains do not arrive. Regions reporting drought:Manakara (2022, 2019, 2020, 2021…), Toliara I (2016–2022 sequences), Toliara II (2019 drought sequence). Agroforestry shade trees (trees that protect crops from heat and wind), mulching (covering soil so it stays moist), soil conservation (protecting soil from erosion), improved seeds, small‑scale irrigation. Excess Rainfall / Flooding Worst excess rainfall years in Manakara show March spikes (maize planting) and May spikes (rice flowering). Flood EAP trigger (150 mm/2 weeks) rarely activates in Toliara despite community reports. Farmers report losses when maize planting(March) and rice flowering (May) coincide with heavy rainfall. Regions: Manakara (frequent), Toliara I (Jan–Feb excess), Toliara II (occasional). Drainage, agroforestry buffers (trees that reduce wind and water damage), soil cover (protect soil with leaves or mulch (covering soil with plant material)), anticipatory action (early seeds, pumps). Cyclones 2022 sequence: Ana → Batsirai → Emnati caused flooding, wind damage, and crop destruction. Cyclone EAP triggers when winds exceed 118–165 km/h. Farmers report lodging, soil erosion, and crop destruction. Regions: Manakara (Batsirai, Emnati 2022; Freddy 2023), Toliara I (Haruna 2013), Toliara II (Freddy 2023). Agroforestry windbreaks, soil conservation, early warning dissemination. Storms Localized high‑wind and heavy‑rain events occur outside cyclone systems. Households report maize and vegetables damaged by strong winds and heavy rain. Regions: Manakara, Toliara I, Toliara II. Windbreaks, agroforestry. Pests / Crop Disease Pest outbreaks follow rainfall anomalies; locust outbreaks documented after wet soils (e.g., 2013 locust outbreak). Farmers report vegetables, maize, and cowpea affected by pests during drought and post‑storm periods. Regions: All three. Integrated pest management, agroforestry biodiversity. Fires Fires occur during drought years when vegetation is dry. Households report cassava, maize, and dry‑season vegetables affected by fires. Region: Manakara. Soil cover, agroforestry moisture retention (trees help the soil hold water longer).

Note: Hazards affect different stages of the agricultural calendar differently, especially planting and flowering periods.

docs.title: 7.2 Case Study 2 — Pastoralism + Rangeland Management
docs.text:
Tabulation: Climate Hazards Affecting Pastoralist Livelihoods

Climate Hazard Evidence from Climate Analysis Community Feedback Adaptation Plan Drought Forage collapse driven by late onset, early cessation, and short dry spells. 2009 drought sequence: delayed onset → poor Jan–Feb rains → early dry season → herd collapse. Pastoralists report forage exhaustion, livestock weight loss, and distress selling. Regions: Toliara I (worst drought years 2009, 2016–2022), Manakara (multiple drought years), Toliara II (2019 drought). Rotational grazing (move animals so grass regrows), dry‑season reserves (keep one area unused for dry season), fodder trees (trees for emergency animal feed), improved water access (better wells or water points). Excess Rainfall / Flooding Jan–Feb rainfall spikes cause pasture flooding and water contamination. Flood EAP trigger rarely activates in Toliara despite community reports. Pastoralists report livestock disease, flooded grazing areas, and contaminated water points. Regions: Toliara I, Manakara, Toliara II. Water management, fodder reserves (stored feed for animals in bad seasons), early warning for disease outbreaks. Cyclones Cyclone Haruna (2013) flooded Toliara I & II; dyke burst (the flood barrier broke and water rushed out); widespread livestock losses. Cyclone EAP triggers at 118–165 km/h. Pastoralists report damaged shelters, blocked migration routes, and water‑point destruction. Regions: Toliara I (Haruna 2013), Manakara (Batsirai/Emnati 2022), Toliara II (Freddy 2023). Water pans (small ponds for storing water), shallow wells (simple hand‑dug wells for water), early warning. Storms High‑wind and heavy‑rain events outside cyclone systems. Households report animal stress, reduced mobility, and difficulty moving herds. Regions: All three. Shelter reinforcement, early warning. Pests / Disease Wet soils after storms create breeding conditions (e.g., 2013 locust outbreak). Pastoralists report livestock disease outbreaks following wet spells. Regions: All three. Veterinary care, grazing management.

Note: Timing of rainfall is more important than totals — short dry spells or early cessation can collapse forage even in “normal” rainfall years.

docs.title: 7.3 Case Study 3 — Fisheries + Mangrove Systems
docs.text:
Tabulation: Climate Hazards Affecting Coastal Livelihoods

Climate Hazard Evidence from Climate Analysis Community Feedback Adaptation Plan Drought 2019 sequence: delayed onset → Jan–Feb rainfall deficits → rising salinity → reduced freshwater input → mangrove stress → fish availability drops. Fishers report lower catch, species shifts, and income drops. Regions: Toliara II (2019), Toliara I (drought sequences), Manakara (multiple drought years). Mangrove restoration (nursery habitat), aquaculture diversification. Cyclones Major cyclone years: Haruna 2013, Batsirai & Emnati 2022, Freddy 2023. All exceed cyclone EAP wind‑speed thresholds. Communities report gear loss, dangerous sea conditions, mangrove damage, and coastal erosion. Regions: Toliara II (Haruna, Freddy), Manakara (Batsirai, Emnati), Toliara I (Haruna). Mangrove planting, gear protection (keeping fishing tools safe from storms), early storm warnings. Storms Storm systems in the Mozambique Channel bring dangerous seas even without cyclone classification. Fishers report lost fishing days, gear damage, and dangerous conditions. Regions: All three. Gear protection, early warning. Excess Rainfall / Flooding Excess rainfall increases turbidity, reducing visibility and catch. Fishers report low catch and delayed return to fishing after heavy rains. Regions: Toliara II, Toliara I, Manakara. Diversification (seaweed, aquaculture), gear improvements. Pests / Disease (mangrove ecosystems) Mangrove disease risk increases after storms and during salinity stress. Communities report mangrove decline affecting nursery habitat. Regions: Toliara II, Toliara I, Manakara. Mangrove restoration, ecosystem monitoring.

Note: Coastal hazards affect both fishing conditions (days at sea) and ecosystem health (mangrove nursery function, species composition).

docs.title: 1. The Household Economy Analysis Approach
docs.text:
After analyzing the mid-term climate risk scenarios, the community feedback, and the Red Cross adaptation plans, we have identified 3 case studies to span the primary adaptation issues and choices under consideration: Field Agriculture, Pastoralism, and Fisheries. In this section, we present a Household Economy Analysis (HEA) for each of these three case studies, breaking down the feasible conditions for sustainable climate adaptation and how AA could enable them.

The HEA approach is used to understand how climate hazards affect different livelihood systems in Madagascar and how specific adaptation investments can change household outcomes during bad years. Agricultural, pastoral, and coastal livelihoods in southern Madagascar operate within a highly variable climate system where timing matters as much as total rainfall. Farmers depend on the onset of rains, the length of dry spells, and the occurrence of extreme events. Pastoralists depend on forage availability, water access, and herd condition. Coastal households depend on fishing days, mangrove protection, and gear security.

Deliverable C shows that communities’ worst drought years in Manakara are consistently associated with rainfall deficits from January through mid‑February, which overlaps with the yam planting period and the early growth stage of rice. Likewise, the worst excess rainfall years in Manakara correspond to rainfall spikes in March (maize planting) and May (rice flowering). In Toliara, drought impacts are driven by late onset of rains, early cessation, or short dry spells during the early vegetative period, while excess rainfall impacts cluster around January and February. For coastal communities, all major community‑reported storm years—such as Freddy (2023), Emnati and Batsirai (2022), and Haruna (2013)—would have triggered the cyclone EAP based on wind‑speed thresholds, but rainfall intensity and storm surge are not fully captured by wind‑speed alone.

For each livelihood, two anchor years are used: the worst overall community‑reported year and the worst community‑reported drought year. These years are drawn directly from community reporting.

docs.title: 1.2 Overview of Approach
docs.text:
Each case study shows what those years meant for that livelihood, then contrasts outcomes for a household with the proposed adaptation investment in place against one without it, for both hazard types. The goal is to make the case for investing now in something that takes time to mature by showing what it changes when a bad year arrives.

The approach includes:

  • Describing the livelihood system
  • Identifying the climate challenges
  • Using community‑reported anchor years
  • Stress‑testing the livelihood under different hazard scenarios
  • Comparing outcomes with and without the adaptation investment
  • Providing facilitation prompts for discussion
  • Presenting structured items that summarize the system, challenges, interventions, feasibility, and links to MRCS programming

Scenario

The scenario considers a typical household in each livelihood system facing:

  • A normal year
  • A drought year
  • An excess rainfall year
  • A cyclone year
  • Repeated bad years
  • A year where forecasts are available before the hazard

The investment does not prevent the bad year; it changes what the household has left when it ends.

docs.title: Case Studies of Specific Livelihood and Adaptation Strategies
Household Economy Analysis
docs.text:
Agricultural households in southern Madagascar operate within a highly variable climate system where timing matters as much as total rainfall. Farmers depend on a mix of rice, maize, cassava, and yams, and their production cycles are tightly linked to the onset of rains, the length of dry spells, and the occurrence of extreme events. Community interviews show that the worst drought years in Manakara consistently coincide with January – mid‑February rainfall deficits, precisely when rice seedlings and yam plantings are most vulnerable.

Deliverable C shows that communities’ worst drought years in Manakara are consistently associated with rainfall deficits from January through mid‑February, which overlaps with the yam planting period and the early growth stage of rice. Likewise, the worst excess rainfall years in Manakara correspond to rainfall spikes in March (maize planting) and May (rice flowering). These sub‑seasonal patterns explain why even “normal” seasonal totals can still produce severe agricultural losses.

Agroforestry systems like fruit trees, shade trees, timber species play a dual role: they diversify income and provide micro‑climate buffering. But they also require land, labor, and time to mature. Under climate change, farmers face increasing uncertainty: dry spells during planting, excess rainfall during flowering, and cyclone‑related wind damage. These shocks reduce yields, increase debt risk, and constrain farmers’ ability to invest in improved seeds or irrigation.

This case study uses a household economy model to stress‑test agricultural livelihoods under different climate scenarios. It examines when agroforestry and soil conservation remain feasible, when they fail, and how risk‑transfer tools (insurance), savings, and anticipatory action can help households make safer decisions. The goal is to show how MRCS programs such as farmer field schools, NbS interventions, and early warning dissemination can support farmers in navigating climate extremes.

docs.title: Anchor Years and Investment Logic
docs.text:
Communities in Manakara identified 2022 as both their worst reported year overall and their worst drought year.

A year like 2022, where the worst overall conditions and the worst drought were reported, highlights exactly what agroforestry is designed to address. When annual crops fail, a household with established tree crops still has income. When soils are damaged, a household with ground cover and root systems recovers faster the following season. The investment does not prevent the bad year, it changes what the household has left when it ends.

However, fruit trees take three to five years to produce meaningful income. A farmer who plants today will not be protected in the next bad year, but can be in the future. Based on the Manakara record, another year like 2022 is not a distant possibility.

docs.title: With and Without Investment
docs.text: Scenario Without Investment With Investment (once established) Worst reported year (2022) Annual crops fail. No alternative income source. Households take on debt or may reduce calories to get through the season. Soil damage from the event reduces next season’s yields as well. One bad year becomes two. Fruit trees and timber provide income that offsets crop losses. The household avoids distress debt and enters the next season with a buffer. Established tree cover and mulching reduce soil damage. Recovery into the next season is faster. Worst drought year (2022) Rainfall deficit reduces yields across all rain-fed crops. The household has no income buffer and no way to extend soil moisture. Shade trees reduce evaporation and buffer soil moisture on the plot. Agroforestry products continue generating income even when annual crops underperform. Worst overall + drought Worst overall conditions and worst drought in the same year deplete household assets with little to no margin for recovery. Diversified income across tree crops and annual crops means no single bad year wipes out the household entirely. Recovery time between shocks is shorter.

docs.title: Structured Items
docs.text:
Livelihood System Dominant crops: rice, maize, cassava, yams Seasonal calendar tied to onset of rains Labor constraints during planting and weeding Limited land availability Agroforestry products: fruit, timber, medicinal plants

Climate Challenge Sub‑seasonal drought around planting Excess rainfall during flowering Cyclone‑related wind + rainfall damage Climate change: increased variability, ENSO/IOD influence

Adaptation Intervention Agroforestry buffers Soil conservation Improved seeds Irrigation Mulching / water retention practices

Stress‑Testing Logic Normal year Drought year Excess rainfall year Cyclone year Repeated bad years Forecast‑informed decisions

Feasibility Considerations Labor requirements Land tradeoffs Upfront costs Time to benefit Gendered labor roles Market access

Links to Red Cross Programming NbS programs Farmer field schools Early warning dissemination Cash‑for‑work for agroforestry

Interaction With Risk‑Transfer & AA Insurance reduces catastrophic losses AA provides early seeds, water pumps Forecasts influence planting decisions Savings buffer moderate losses

docs.title: Household Economy Analysis
docs.text:
Pastoralist households in Toliara rely on cattle, goats, and sheep, moving seasonally across rangelands in search of forage and water. Their livelihood is shaped by the timing of rains, the length of dry seasons, and the availability of grazing reserves. Community data shows that drought years often correspond to forage collapse, even when total rainfall is not extremely low, highlighting the importance of sub‑seasonal timing and distribution of rainfall rather than totals.

Deliverable C indicates that drought impacts in Toliara are not well captured by seasonal rainfall totals. Instead, forage collapse is driven by late onset of rains, early cessation of rains, or short dry spells during the early vegetative period. Communities also consistently identified excess rainfall around January and February as damaging, causing livestock disease, pasture flooding, and water contamination.

Rangeland management like rotational grazing, dry‑season reserves, fodder trees, and improved water access, can significantly reduce losses in bad years. But these interventions require community coordination, labor, and governance structures. Under climate change, hotter dry seasons and erratic rainfall increase pressure on grazing systems, making pastoralists more vulnerable to repeated shocks.

This case study uses a household economy model to stress‑test pastoralist livelihoods under different climate scenarios. It examines when rangeland management remains feasible, when herds fall below recovery thresholds, and how AA, insurance, and savings can help households avoid catastrophic herd losses. The analysis links directly to MRCS programs on livestock health, water access, and community early warning systems.

docs.title: Anchor Years and Investment Logic
docs.text:
Communities in Toliara I identified 2013 as their worst reported year overall and 2009 as their worst drought year.

The gap between 2009 and 2013 is four years. A household that lost herd condition in 2009 and had not rebuilt rangeland management systems was exposed again in 2013, this time from a different hazard. Rangeland management does not eliminate losses in bad years, but it changes the starting point. A household with dry‑season reserves, rotational grazing, and fodder trees enters a bad year with more options.

In this context, rangeland management refers to a combined strategy of rotational grazing, resting one grazing block per season, establishing dry‑season reserves, planting fodder trees for emergency feed, and improving water access points such as shallow wells or protected pans.

Building these systems takes time. A rested grazing block needs time to regenerate. Fodder trees also take time to establish. Community governance for rotational grazing requires coordination before any individual household benefits. This work has to happen in manageable years, not during a crisis.

docs.title: With and Without Investment
docs.text: Scenario Without Investment With Investment (once established) Worst reported year (2013) No mechanism to protect animals when hazard conditions develop. Herd losses occur before the household can respond. No early warning response capacity. Rotational management and reserve infrastructure mean the household can act before conditions become critical. Animals are moved to rested areas before communal land is exhausted. Reserve infrastructure makes forecast information actionable. Worst drought year (2009) Forage exhausted before the dry season ends. No reserves. Household sells animals at low prices or watches herd collapse. No fodder trees or supplementary feed. Dry‑season grazing reserves provide forage when communal land is depleted. Distress selling is avoided. Fodder trees provide supplementary feed. Improved water access reduces pressure on animals during dry months. Herd condition is maintained longer.

docs.title: Structured Items
docs.text:
Livelihood System Cattle, goats, sheep Grazing calendar Water access points Migration routes Fodder availability

Climate Challenge Drought → forage collapse Excess rainfall → livestock disease Cyclones → infrastructure damage Climate change → hotter dry seasons, erratic rains

Adaptation Intervention Rangeland rotation Dry‑season grazing reserves Water pans / shallow wells Fodder trees Veterinary care

Stress‑Testing Logic One bad year Two consecutive bad years Delayed rains Early cessation of rains Forecast‑informed migration decisions

Feasibility Considerations Communal land governance Conflict risk Labor requirements Water access Herd size thresholds

Links to Red Cross Programming Rangeland management training Livestock health campaigns Water access projects Community early warning systems

Interaction With Risk‑Transfer & AA Insurance protects herd value AA provides early feed/water Forecasts guide herd movement Savings buffer veterinary costs

docs.title: Household Economy Analysis
docs.text:
Coastal households in southern Madagascar depend on nearshore fishing, mangrove harvesting, and small‑scale marine resources. Their income varies with tides, seasons, and storm activity. Community reports show that the worst storm years correspond to cyclone tracks intersecting fishing zones, causing gear loss, dangerous sea conditions, and mangrove damage. Excess rainfall also affects fishing by increasing turbidity and reducing catch.

Deliverable C shows that all major community‑reported storm years—such as Freddy (2023), Emnati and Batsirai (2022), and Haruna (2013)—would have triggered the cyclone EAP based on wind‑speed thresholds. However, the analysis also highlights that rainfall intensity and storm surge, which strongly affect fishing conditions and mangrove ecosystems, are not fully captured by wind‑speed alone.

Mangrove ecosystems provide critical nursery habitat, storm buffering, and wood resources. But they are vulnerable to both overharvesting and climate extremes. Under climate change, warming waters, shifting species, and more intense cyclones increase uncertainty for fishing households.

This case study uses a household economy model to stress‑test fishing livelihoods under different climate scenarios. It examines when mangrove restoration and gear improvements remain feasible, when storms overwhelm coping capacity, and how AA, insurance, and savings can reduce losses. It links directly to MRCS coastal early warning systems, mangrove restoration programs, and community disaster preparedness.

docs.title: Anchor Years and Investment Logic
docs.text:
Communities in Toliara II identified 2019 as both their worst reported year overall and their worst drought year.

A household with no alternative income, no gear protection, and degraded mangroves around them has nothing to fall back on at any point in the year. Mangrove restoration takes time before a newly planted stand provides meaningful coastal buffering and nursery habitat. This means investments made today will not be fully in place for the next 2019. But without starting now, they will not be in place for the 2019 after that either.

In this context, aquaculture refers to small‑scale seaweed farming or nearshore fish‑pond systems that provide income even when fishing days are lost due to storms, turbidity, or dangerous sea conditions. Mangrove work includes both restoration of degraded stands and planting of new mangrove seedlings; both approaches take years before providing full coastal buffering and nursery habitat.

docs.title: With and Without Investment
docs.text: Scenario Without Investment With Investment (once established) Worst reported year (2019) Hazard conditions prevent fishing for extended periods. Gear or boat damage adds recovery costs. No alternative income during this window. Household absorbs the full loss. Degraded mangroves provide less protection to the coastline. Diversified income from seaweed or aquaculture provides a buffer when fishing is not possible. Household resumes normal activity sooner. Restored mangrove systems buffer coastal energy and recover faster between storms. Worst drought year (2019) Salinity changes and reduced freshwater input shift fish availability. Catch drops and income falls with no buffer. Restored mangrove systems are more resilient to salinity stress. Nursery habitat quality is maintained, supporting more stable fish populations near the coast. Worst overall + drought Worst overall conditions and worst drought in the same year leave the household with no recovery window. Assets are depleted going into the next season. Diversified income, gear protection, and healthy mangroves mean both hazard types are survivable. The household retains productive capacity into the following season.

docs.title: Structured Items
docs.text:
Livelihood System Nearshore fishing Seasonal fish availability Mangrove harvesting Boat/gear constraints Gendered division of labor

Climate Challenge Cyclones → storm surge, gear loss Excess rainfall → turbidity, reduced catch Drought → salinity changes Climate change → warming waters, shifting species

Adaptation Intervention Mangrove restoration Sustainable gear Early warning for storms Diversification (seaweed, aquaculture)

Stress‑Testing Logic Cyclone year Excess rainfall year Repeated storm years Forecast‑informed fishing decisions

Feasibility Considerations Gear cost Mangrove access rights Market access Gender roles Time to ecological recovery

Links to Red Cross Programming Mangrove restoration Coastal early warning systems Cash‑for‑work Community disaster preparedness

Interaction With Risk‑Transfer & AA AA provides early storm alerts Insurance protects gear Savings buffer lost income Forecasts guide fishing days

docs.title: 4b. Stress-Testing AA and Adaptation Under Climate Change
2. Scoping Analysis of Adaptation and AA Potential
2.1 Scoping Analysis Tool User Guide
docs.text:
Access the tool here: https://ccsfist.github.io/mrcs_scoping/

The Scoping Analysis Tool simulates the household economy of a typical community member deciding how to invest in adaptation. The subject has to make decisions about livelihood and risk mitigation investments in advance of the season, which has some probability of being either a good year (in which they receive returns to their livelihood investment) or a bad year (in which they don’t). The tool presents outcomes during a good year and a bad year, as well as the long-term average outcome (i.e., the expected value).

The basic structure of this household economy model is identical to that presented in the previous section. This tool simplifies the model slightly, by collapsing non-farm income (e.g., savings, nature-based solutions) into a single “outside income” category and focusing on analysis of the enabling conditions for sustainable adaptation.

The Scoping Analysis Tool has three parts. The center pane presents the key messages about the business model under consideration. The sliders on the left-hand pane allow the user to adjust the parameters of the business model and explore how the key messages change. Finally, the “Simulate Strategy” button allows the user to put the parameters of the chosen business model into a risk scenario that could be presented to communities, which we will discuss further in the following section.

The Key Messages

The top of the page shows the key messages about this business model. The first is whether investing in improved livelihoods and (if applicable) insurance would result in a higher income on average. The second is whether the subject would have enough income during bad years to break even. The third is the insurance premium for the policy under consideration.

Below the key messages, the center pane shows a summary table of the outcomes under each combination of strategies - investing in improved inputs vs not, and purchasing insurance vs not. The tables below show a detailed breakdown of the costs and benefits under each strategy. This includes the scenario in which there is a bad year, but the insurance fails to pay out (i.e., basis risk).

The Household Economy Model Parameters

The left-hand pane allows the user to adjust each of the parameters of the household economy model, and observe how outcomes change. This can be used to test the sensitivity of a given adaptation theory of change to various assumptions. The functioning of each parameter is described below, using the case of farming as an example:

  • Good Year Base Value: The value of income from a good harvest year, in units of local currency. This determines the rest of the numbers used in the scenario. Unless specified otherwise, all other parameters are expressed as percentages of the base value (with the equivalent value in currency units shown in blue above the respective slider).
  • Improved Cost: The cost of improved inputs.
  • Yield Gain: The improvement in income in a good year from improved inputs.
  • Typical Payout: The value of an insurance payout during bad years.
  • Loading Fee: The additional cost of purchasing insurance, reflecting fees and capital costs. Expressed as a percentage of the pure risk premium (i.e., typical payout * prob. of bad year).
  • Outside Savings: The value of non-farm sources of income (which could include savings, nature-based solutions, etc.)
  • Bad Year Prob.: The probability of a bad weather year.
  • Failed Payout Prob.: The probability that insurance will fail to pay out, conditional on it being a bad weather year.

The Risk Scenario Simulator

To translate the parameters of the chosen business model into a scenario exercise that could be played with communities, press the “Simulate Strategy” button on the bottom left-hand side of the screen. You will see a “Simulation” pop-up like the one above.

This simulation is broken into three rounds, following a simplified version of the structure discussed yesterday. Each round introduces an additional increment of complexity in the choices the player faces.

  • Round 1: Choice - The user chooses whether or not to take out a loan to invest in improved inputs.
  • Round 2: Coverage - The user faces two choices: a) Whether or not to invest in improved inputs (same as round 1), and b) whether or not to purchase weather insurance.
  • Round 3: Failed Payout - The same choices as Round 2, but with the added complexity that insurance may fail to pay out during some bad years (basis risk).

Use the “Package” and “Insurance” dropdowns on the left-hand side to toggle between each of players’ choice options (invest in inputs vs not, buy insurance vs not).

Press the “Run Season” option to simulate a seasonal outcome, using the probabilities of a good and bad year determined in the Parameters pane (shown in the top left hand corner of the Simulation pane for reference). Repeated runs of the season will result in different outcomes. This can be used to simulate possibilities when discussing the risk scenarios with communities.

To move from round to round, press the buttons on the center top part of the pane. To close out of the Simulation pane, press the X in the top right.

Customizing the Tool

You can change any of the text in the parameter boxes by mousing over it, deleting the text that is currently there, and writing your own. This allows you to customize the business model to different contexts, e.g. pastoralists instead of farmers. You can also customize the title and key message text.

To save your customizations, along with the chosen parameter values, press the “Save Current Version” button on the bottom left hand side of the screen. This will allow you to save the current customized version of the scoping analysis tool as an .html file.

docs.title: 2.2 Exploring & Refining Scoping Analysis
docs.text:
Using this framework, we can assess the enabling conditions for the community-led adaptation strategies we discussed on day 1.

This is a form of feasibility analysis for different adaptation options.

docs.title: 4b. Stress-Testing AA and Adaptation Under Climate Change (FR)
2. Analyse préliminaire du potentiel d'adaptation et de l'évaluation de l'impact
2.1 Guide de l'utilisateur de l'outil d'analyse de la portée
docs.text:
Accédez à l'outil ici : https://ccsfist.github.io/mrcs_scoping/ L'outil d'analyse préliminaire simule l'économie domestique d'un membre typique de la communauté qui doit décider comment investir dans l'adaptation. L'individu doit prendre des décisions concernant ses investissements dans les moyens de subsistance et l'atténuation des risques avant le début de la saison, qui a une certaine probabilité d'être soit une bonne année (au cours de laquelle il tire un bénéfice de son investissement dans les moyens de subsistance), soit une mauvaise année (au cours de laquelle il n'en tire aucun bénéfice). L'outil présente les résultats pour une bonne année et une mauvaise année, ainsi que le résultat moyen à long terme (c'est-à-dire la valeur attendue). La structure de base de ce modèle économique est identique à celle présentée dans les scénarios de risque abordés hier. Cet outil simplifie légèrement le modèle de scénarios de risque, en regroupant les revenus non agricoles (par exemple, l'épargne, les solutions fondées sur la nature) dans une seule catégorie intitulée « revenus extérieurs » et en se concentrant sur l'analyse des conditions propices à une adaptation durable. L'outil d'analyse préliminaire comporte trois parties. Le volet central présente les messages clés relatifs au modèle économique étudié. Les curseurs situés dans le volet de gauche permettent à l'utilisateur d'ajuster les paramètres du modèle économique et d'observer l'évolution des messages clés. Enfin, le bouton « Simuler la stratégie » permet à l'utilisateur d'intégrer les paramètres du modèle économique choisi dans un scénario de risque susceptible d'être présenté aux communautés, conformément à la structure évoquée hier.

Les messages clés

En haut de la page figurent les points clés concernant ce modèle économique. Le premier consiste à déterminer si investir dans l'amélioration des moyens de subsistance et (le cas échéant) dans une assurance permettrait d'obtenir un revenu moyen plus élevé. Le deuxième consiste à déterminer si le bénéficiaire disposerait d'un revenu suffisant pendant les mauvaises années pour atteindre le seuil de rentabilité. Le troisième concerne la prime d'assurance pour le contrat envisagé.

Sous les messages clés, le volet central présente un tableau récapitulatif des résultats pour chaque combinaison de stratégies : investir ou non dans des intrants améliorés, et souscrire ou non une assurance. Les tableaux ci-dessous fournissent une ventilation détaillée des coûts et des avantages pour chaque stratégie. Cela inclut le scénario dans lequel la récolte est mauvaise, mais où l'assurance ne verse pas d'indemnité (c'est-à-dire le risque de base).

Les paramètres du modèle économique

Le volet de gauche permet à l'utilisateur de modifier chacun des paramètres du modèle économique et d'observer l'évolution des résultats. Cette fonctionnalité peut être utilisée pour tester la sensibilité d'une théorie du changement donnée à diverses hypothèses. Le fonctionnement de chaque paramètre est décrit ci-dessous, en prenant comme exemple le scénario d'exploitation agricole par défaut : Valeur de référence d'une bonne année : Valeur des revenus générés par une bonne année de récolte, exprimée en unités monétaires locales. Ce chiffre détermine tous les autres chiffres utilisés dans le scénario. Sauf indication contraire, tous les autres paramètres sont exprimés en pourcentage de la valeur de référence (la valeur équivalente en unités monétaires étant indiquée en bleu au-dessus du curseur correspondant). Coût des intrants améliorés : Le coût des intrants améliorés. Gain de rendement : Augmentation des revenus lors d'une bonne année grâce à l'utilisation d'intrants de meilleure qualité. Indemnité type : Le montant de l'indemnité versée par l'assurance lors des mauvaises années. Frais de souscription : Coût supplémentaire lié à la souscription d'une assurance, tenant compte des frais et des coûts de capital. Exprimé en pourcentage de la prime de risque pur (c'est-à-dire le montant typique de l'indemnisation * la probabilité d'une mauvaise année). Économies externes : La valeur des sources de revenus non agricoles (qui peuvent inclure l'épargne, les solutions fondées sur la nature, etc.) Probabilité d'une année de mauvaises conditions météorologiques : La probabilité qu'une année soit marquée par de mauvaises conditions météorologiques. Probabilité de non-indemnisation : Probabilité que l'assurance ne verse pas d'indemnisation, à condition que l'année soit marquée par des conditions météorologiques défavorables.

Le simulateur de scénarios de risque

Pour traduire les paramètres du modèle économique choisi en un exercice de simulation pouvant être réalisé avec les communautés, cliquez sur le bouton « Simuler la stratégie » situé en bas à gauche de l'écran. Une fenêtre contextuelle « Simulation » s'affichera, comme celle ci-dessus. Cette simulation se déroule en trois manches, selon une version simplifiée de la structure évoquée hier. Chaque manche apporte un niveau supplémentaire de complexité aux choix auxquels le joueur est confronté. 1er tour : Choix – L'utilisateur décide s'il souhaite ou non contracter un emprunt pour investir dans des intrants de meilleure qualité. 2e étape : couverture - L'utilisateur doit faire face à deux choix : a) décider s'il souhaite ou non investir dans des intrants de meilleure qualité (comme lors de la 1re étape), et b) décider s'il souhaite ou non souscrire une assurance contre les aléas climatiques. 3e tour : absence de versement – Les mêmes choix qu'au 2e tour, mais avec une complexité supplémentaire : l'assurance peut ne pas verser d'indemnité lors de certaines années difficiles (risque de base). Utilisez les menus déroulants « Forfait » et « Assurance » situés à gauche pour basculer entre les différentes options proposées aux joueurs (investir dans les intrants ou non, souscrire une assurance ou non). Cliquez sur l'option « Simuler la saison » pour simuler les résultats d'une saison, en utilisant les probabilités d'une bonne ou d'une mauvaise année définies dans le volet « Paramètres » (affichées dans le coin supérieur gauche du volet « Simulation » à titre de référence). Des simulations répétées de la saison donneront lieu à des résultats différents. Cette fonctionnalité peut être utilisée pour simuler différentes possibilités lors des discussions sur les scénarios de risque avec les communautés. Pour passer d'une étape à l'autre, appuyez sur les boutons situés en haut au centre du volet. Pour fermer le volet Simulation, cliquez sur le X en haut à droite. Personnalisation de l'outil Vous pouvez modifier le texte figurant dans les champs de paramètres en passant la souris dessus, en supprimant le texte actuel et en saisissant le vôtre. Cela vous permet d'adapter le modèle économique à différents contextes, par exemple en remplaçant « agriculteurs » par « éleveurs ». Vous pouvez également personnaliser le titre et le texte du message clé. Pour enregistrer vos personnalisations ainsi que les valeurs des paramètres sélectionnés, cliquez sur le bouton « Enregistrer la version actuelle » situé en bas à gauche de l'écran. Cela vous permettra d'enregistrer la version personnalisée actuelle de l'outil d'analyse de périmètre sous forme de fichier .html.

This is a form of feasibility analysis for different adaptation options.
